questions.question_text: An Iowa broker wants to open a trust account at an online-only bank headquartered in another state. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, if the institution is federally insured
distractors: No, the account must be at an Iowa institution
No, online-only banks are categorically prohibited
Only if the Commission grants a waiver
questions.explanation: Iowa Code §543B.46(1) requires the trust account to be maintained in a federally insured depository institution. Neither the statute nor IAC 193E—13.1 imposes an in-Iowa location requirement, so an online-only or out-of-state bank may be used as long as it carries federal deposit insurance (FDIC or NCUA) and the broker files the required Consent to Examine and Audit Trust Account form with the Commission.

questions.question_text: What type of bank account must an Iowa broker use for a trust account?
distractors: Non-interest demand deposit only
Certificate of deposit
Money market savings without check access
questions.explanation: Iowa Code §543B.46(1) requires the broker's trust account to be an interest-bearing account in a federally insured depository institution. Interest earned is transferred quarterly to the Iowa Finance Authority for the state housing trust fund (Iowa Code §16.181) unless the buyer and seller agree in writing to direct it elsewhere. Fixed-term accounts like CDs cannot be used without the written consent of the parties whose funds are deposited (IAC 193E—13.1(1)"b").

questions.question_text: A salesperson in an Iowa brokerage wants to deposit earnest money into the trust account. Under Iowa law, who may access the trust account?
questions.correct_answer: The broker or authorized designees only
distractors: Any licensed salesperson in the office
The listing agent handling the transaction
The office manager regardless of licensure
questions.explanation: Iowa law restricts trust account access to the broker and specifically authorized designees. A salesperson cannot deposit or withdraw funds unless the broker has formally designated that person as authorized. Simply holding a license or managing a transaction does not grant access.

questions.question_text: An Iowa trust account is labeled 'Operating Account' at the bank. Does this comply with Iowa requirements?
questions.correct_answer: No, it must be designated trust or escrow
distractors: Yes, the label does not matter legally
Yes, if the broker tracks funds separately
Only if the bank knows its purpose
questions.explanation: Iowa requires the trust account to be specifically designated as a 'trust' or 'escrow' account at the financial institution. An account labeled 'Operating Account' does not meet this requirement, even if the broker internally tracks the funds separately.

questions.question_text: Which entity must insure the financial institution where an Iowa broker maintains a trust account?
distractors: The Iowa Real Estate Commission
A private surety company
The Iowa Division of Banking only
questions.explanation: Iowa requires trust accounts to be held at federally insured financial institutions, meaning the institution must carry FDIC or NCUA insurance. The Real Estate Commission, private sureties, and state banking divisions do not provide the required deposit insurance.

questions.question_text: Under Rule 193E-13.1, an Iowa broker receives a fully executed purchase agreement on Monday. By when must earnest money be deposited?
questions.correct_answer: Within 5 banking days of last signature
distractors: Within 3 banking days of last signature
By the next business day
Within 10 calendar days of acceptance
questions.explanation: Rule 193E-13.1 requires deposit of earnest money within 5 banking days after the last signature of acceptance, unless the contract specifies a different timeline. The clock starts when the agreement is fully executed with all required signatures.

questions.question_text: An Iowa broker deposits $200 of personal funds into the trust account to cover monthly bank service charges. Is this permissible?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, nominal personal funds for bank fees are OK
distractors: No, any personal funds constitute commingling
Only with written Commission approval
Yes, up to $1,000 is always permitted
questions.explanation: Iowa trust account rules prohibit commingling but allow a broker to maintain nominal personal funds in the trust account to cover bank service charges. This small amount prevents the account from falling below zero due to fees. There is no specific dollar cap like $1,000.

questions.question_text: Two parties dispute ownership of earnest money held in an Iowa broker's trust account. Neither will consent to release. What is the broker's proper action?
questions.correct_answer: File an interpleader action with the court
distractors: Return the funds to the buyer automatically
Split the funds equally between parties
Hold indefinitely until parties agree
questions.explanation: When parties dispute earnest money and will not provide written consent for disbursement, Iowa law provides for interpleader. The broker files an interpleader action, depositing the funds with the court and allowing a judge to determine proper distribution.

questions.question_text: Before closing, an Iowa seller asks the broker to release earnest money to pay for a home inspection. What is required?
questions.correct_answer: Informed written consent from all parties
distractors: Verbal approval from the buyer only
Broker discretion if the amount is small
Automatic release if inspection is in the contract
questions.explanation: Iowa trust account rules prohibit disbursement before closing without informed written consent from all parties to the transaction. The broker cannot release funds based solely on verbal approval, their own judgment, or contract provisions without explicit written authorization.

questions.question_text: A purchase agreement states earnest money must be deposited within 3 banking days. The standard Iowa rule allows 5 banking days. Which deadline controls?
distractors: The 5-day statutory default applies
Whichever is longer benefits the broker
The Commission must approve shorter timelines
questions.explanation: Rule 193E-13.1 sets a default of 5 banking days but explicitly allows the contract to specify otherwise. When a purchase agreement sets a shorter deadline of 3 banking days, the contract terms control and the broker must comply with the agreed-upon timeline.

questions.question_text: How long must an Iowa broker retain trust account records after a transaction closes?
distractors: 3 years
7 years
10 years
questions.explanation: Rule 193E-13.5 requires Iowa brokers to retain trust account records for 5 years after closing. This includes all transaction records, deposit slips, disbursement records, and bank statements associated with the trust account.

questions.question_text: The Iowa Real Estate Commission arrives unannounced at a brokerage to review trust account records. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, the Commission may audit at any time
distractors: No, 30 days written notice is required
Only with a court order
Only during scheduled annual reviews
questions.explanation: Iowa law authorizes the Real Estate Commission to audit broker trust accounts at any time without prior notice. Brokers consent to this oversight as a condition of holding a license and accepting client funds.

questions.question_text: An Iowa broker who accepts earnest money must have which form on file with the Commission?
questions.correct_answer: Consent to Examine and Audit Trust Account
distractors: Trust Account Status Affidavit
Broker Financial Disclosure Statement
Annual Trust Account Certification
questions.explanation: Iowa brokers who accept earnest money must file a 'Consent to Examine and Audit Trust Account' form. Brokers who do not accept earnest money file a 'Trust Account Status Affidavit' instead. These are distinct forms with different purposes.

questions.question_text: An Iowa broker does not accept earnest money in any transactions. What form must this broker file?
distractors: Consent to Examine and Audit Trust Account
Earnest Money Exemption Certificate
No form is required in this situation
questions.explanation: Iowa brokers who do not accept earnest money must file a 'Trust Account Status Affidavit' confirming they do not hold client funds. This is different from the 'Consent to Examine and Audit' form required of brokers who do accept earnest money.

questions.question_text: During an audit, the Commission finds an Iowa broker destroyed trust account records from a transaction that closed 4 years ago. What is the finding?
distractors: No violation since 3 years have passed
Acceptable if digital copies were backed up
Minor infraction with no disciplinary action
questions.explanation: Rule 193E-13.5 requires 5-year retention of trust account records after closing. Destroying records from a transaction that closed only 4 years ago violates this rule and can result in disciplinary action by the Commission.
